title: Engineering a driven-dissipative bath of altermagnetic quantum magnons for controlling classical dynamics of spins hosting spin waves, domain walls, or skyrmions
abstract: Using Schwinger-Keldysh field theory (SKFT), we engineer a dissipative and driven (i.e., out of equilibrium) bosonic bath acting on classical localized spins within a ferromagnetic insulator (FI) layer whose dynamics is governed by the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation, as is usually assumed in spintronics and magnonics. The bosonic bath is comprised of quantum magnons within a layer of altermagnetic insulator (AMI) that is attached to a conventional FI layer, often one of the key ingredients within spintronic and magnonic multilayers, so that interaction between slow classical (in the FI layer) and fast quantum (in the AMI layer) localized spins ensues. Such a bath, including its driving to produce a nonequilibrium distribution of altermagnetic magnons, generates a rich structure of the SKFT-derived extended LLG equation for classical spins within the FI layer. Our LLG equation contains two damping terms, both of which are spatially nonlocal and anisotropic, while one of them is also intrinsically non-Markovian, i.e., nonlocal in time. We demonstrate how to exploit these terms for tuning spintronic and magnonic effects within the FI layer of AMI/FI bilayers that involve spin wave or domain wall propagation, as well as skyrmion annihilation.
summary: This paper proposes a method to engineer complex, controllable damping in magnetic layers by coupling them to a driven-dissipative bath of altermagnetic magnons. It shows that this coupling introduces non-local and non-Markovian effects that can be used to tune the motion of domain walls and the stability of skyrmions. This provides a new theoretical toolkit for active control in spintronic and magnonic devices.
main_problem: The challenge of controlling and engineering magnetization damping in spintronics and magnonics by using a driven-dissipative bath of quantum magnons.
main_result: The authors derive an extended Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation containing spatially nonlocal, anisotropic, and non-Markovian damping terms induced by an altermagnetic insulator layer.
method: Schwinger-Keldysh field theory (SKFT) is used to integrate out the quantum magnon bath, combined with Holstein-Primakoff and Bogoliulyov transformations.
model_or_system: An AMI/FI bilayer consisting of an altermagnetic insulator (AMI) layer hosting quantum magnons and a ferromagnetic insulator (FI) layer with classical localized spins, coupled via interlayer exchange $J_I$.
key_observables: Spin wave spectrum (frequency and damping), domain wall velocity, skyrmion stability/annihilation, and the spatial dependence of the nonlocal torque.
important_parameters: Altermagnetic strength ($\delta$), interlayer coupling ($J_I$), driving frequency ($\Omega_c$), temperature ($T$), and intrinsic Gilbert damping ($\alpha_G$).
main_assumptions: Small interlayer coupling (second-order expansion in $J_I$), long-wavelength approximation for AMI magnon dispersion, and neglect of fluctuations quadratic in quantum fields.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the bilayer schematic and lattice structure; Fig 2 displays the spin wave spectrum and non-Markovian damping; Fig 3 illustrates the spatial dependence and anisotropy of the nonlocal torque.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of damping control, defines the AMI/FI bilayer model, applies SKFT to derive the effective action and extended LLG equation, and then demonstrates the physical consequences for spin waves, domain walls, and skyrmions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic view of the AMI/FI bilayer, where the AMI layer hosts a bath of altermagnetic quantum magnons arising as the low-energy excitations of the Heisenberg Hamil- tonian. The exchange coupling between NN localized spins (dotted black lines) is J1 > 0, while the one between NNN spins is J2(1 + δ) (solid lines) or J2(1 −δ) (dashed lines) de- pending [27, 28] on the direction and sublattice (blue for sub- lattice A and red for sublattice B). (b) Spin-split spectrum of chiral altermagnetic magnons [29] computed for J2 = 0.2J1, δ = 0.3, and magnetic anisotropy K = 0.1J1. The inset of panel (b) labels high-symmetry points in the first BZ.

FIG. 2. (a) SW spectrum of FI layer (black lines) when sub- jected to interaction with the bath of quantum magnons in AMI layer [Fig. 1(a)]. At the crossing points, spikes emerge in the upper SW band, while SW damping increases (b) with intricate dependence on wavevector amplitude and direction. FI parameters are chosen as J = J1 and K = −0.3J.

FIG. 3. Spatial dependence of nonlocal function Λnn′ in Eq. (1), as induced by the bath of AMI quantum magnons in (a),(b) equilibrium at temperature, T = J1; or (c),(d) out of equilibrium due to driving coherent magnons at frequency Ωc = 2J1. Darker colors indicate stronger coupling of spins at relative separation r = rn −rn′. Panels in the left (right) column depict coupling within sublattice A (B), while inter- sublattice coupling is not shown. AMI parameters are chosen as J2 = 0.2J1, δ = 0.3 and K = −0.1J1.

FIG. 5. Spatial distribution of localized spins Sz n(t) at differ- ent times within the FI layer subjected to the bath of AMI quantum magnons [Fig. 1]. The bath is driven at frequency (a)–(d) Ωc = 1.5J1; or (e)–(h) Ωc = 2J1.

FIG. 4. Spatial distribution of localized spins Sz n(t) within the FI layer at a time t = 100/J after its DW propagation sub- jected to the bath of AMI quantum magnons [Fig. 1] that is in (a) thermal equilibrium; or (b) driven at frequency Ωc = 2J1. Initial and final DW positions, in the absence of the AMI layer, are indicated in dashed and solid white lines, respec- tively. Panels (c) and (d) show the relative change of DW velocity as a function of its direction of propagation when AMI is in vs. out of equilibrium, respectively.

Spintronics.note: The paper directly addresses the interface of quantum-magnon baths and magnetic layers, providing a new theoretical toolkit for controlling spin waves and skyrmions via driven-dissipative coupling.
